message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To investigate the clinical characteristics and outcomes of Coronavirus Disease of 2019 (COVID-19) patients complicated with venous thromboembolism (VTE)</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We performed a comprehensive literature search of several databases to find studies that assessed VTE in hospitalized COVID-19 patients with a primary outcome of all-cause mortality and secondary outcomes of intensive care unit (ICU) admission and mechanical ventilation. We also evaluated the clinical characteristics of VTE sufferers.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Eight studies have been included with a total of 1237 pooled subjects. Venous thromboembolism was associated with higher mortality (RR 2.48 (1.35, 4.55),
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.003;
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    5%,
                    <jats:italic>p=</jats:italic>
                    0.35) after we performed sensitivity analysis, ICU admission (RR 2.32 (1.53, 3.52),
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <0.0001;
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    80%,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <0.0001), and mechanical ventilation need (RR 2.73 (1.56, 4.78),
                    <jats:italic>p=</jats:italic>
                    0.0004;
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    77%,
                    <jats:italic>p=</jats:italic>
                    0.001). Furthermore, it was also associated to male gender (RR 1.21 (1.08, 1.35),
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.0007;
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    12%,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.34), higher white blood cells count (MD 1.24 (0.08, 2.41), 0.04;
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    0%; 0.26), D-dimer (MD 4.49 (2.74, 6.25),
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <0.00001;
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    67%,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.009) and LDH levels (MD 70.93 (19.33, 122.54),
                    <jats:italic>p<</jats:italic>
                    0.007;
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    21%, p=0.28). In addition, after sensitivity analysis was conducted, VTE also associated with older age (MD 2.79 (0.06, 5.53),
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.05;
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    25%,
                    <jats:italic>p=</jats:italic>
                    0.24) and higher CRP levels (MD 2.57 (0.88, 4.26);
                    <jats:italic>p=</jats:italic>
                    0.003;
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    0%,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.96).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Venous thromboembolism in COVID-19 patients was associated with increased mortality, ICU admission, and mechanical ventilation requirement. Male gender, older age, higher levels of biomarkers, including WBC count, D-Dimer, and LDH were also being considerably risks for developing VTE in COVID-19 patients during hospitalization.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Clinical Characteristics and Outcomes of Venous Thromboembolism in Patients Hospitalized for COVID-19: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
